summary: Physicists from West Virginia University and the University of Chicago Pritzker School of Molecular Engineering have shown that adjusting the ratio of tellurium and selenium in iron telluride selenide thin films enables the tuning of material between different quantum phases, including the highly sought-after topological superconductor state. Published in Nature Communications, the study highlights how changing electron correlations—modulated by this ratio—controls material properties essential for error-resistant quantum computing. Assistant professor Subhasish Mandal notes that these electron interactions act as a "powerful and previously underappreciated tool for engineering topological quantum matter." This research demonstrates that quantum materials can be actively tuned for more robust quantum devices, addressing a key challenge in building reliable quantum computers.
